title: Regions, zones, geographies & region pairs
summary: Azure's global infrastructure is layered: geographies hold regions, regions hold availability zones, zones hold datacenters. Where you deploy decides latency, legal data residency, and how much failure you survive — so physical geography is an architectural decision, not a dropdown.
analogy: Think of a bank: a geography is the country it operates in (its laws), a region is a city branch network, an availability zone is a separate vault building wired to its own power, and a region pair is a sister city you replicate the ledger to in case the whole city floods.
diagram:
flowchart TD
  Geo[Geography - e.g. United States] --> R1[Region: East US]
  Geo --> R2[Region: West US - paired]
  R1 -.region pair.-> R2
  R1 --> Z1[Availability Zone 1]
  R1 --> Z2[Availability Zone 2]
  R1 --> Z3[Availability Zone 3]
  Z1 --> D1[Datacenter(s) - own power, cooling, network]
body:
## First principles

Software feels abstract, but it runs on a physical machine in a physical building in a physical country. Three unavoidable realities flow from that: **light is slow** (distance to your users adds latency), **buildings fail** (power, cooling, fiber, floods, fire), and **countries have laws** (where data may legally live). Azure's global infrastructure is the layered answer to all three — and choosing where to deploy is how you trade them off.

## Why it exists

One giant datacenter would be a disaster on every axis: far from most users, a single point of failure, and bound to one country's law. So Azure is deliberately carved into a hierarchy of locations, each layer solving one of those problems:

- Put capacity **near users** → low latency.
- Spread capacity across **independent buildings** → survive a building failure.
- Group capacity by **country/legal boundary** → meet data-residency rules.

Before this, surviving a datacenter fire meant *you* building a second datacenter in another city and replicating to it by hand. Azure's geography layers turn that into a deployment choice.

## How it works

Four nested concepts, biggest to smallest:

- **Geography** — a discrete market, usually a country or region of the world (e.g. United States, Europe, India), that defines a **data-residency and compliance boundary**. Data in a geography generally stays in that geography.
- **Region** — a set of datacenters within a metro area, deployed within a latency-defined perimeter (e.g. *East US*, *West Europe*). This is the level you actually pick when you deploy. Azure has **60+ regions** worldwide and keeps adding more.
- **Availability Zone (AZ)** — one or more datacenters *within a region* with **independent power, cooling, and networking**. Zone-enabled regions have at least **three** zones. Spreading replicas across zones means a single building failure doesn't take your app down.
- **Region pair** — historically, each region was paired with another in the same geography (typically 300+ miles apart) for geo-redundancy: paired regions get staggered platform updates and are the target for some services' geo-replication. **Note the 2025 shift:** many newer regions are *non-paired*, and Microsoft now guides you to architect for **availability zones first** for high availability, using a second region only for true regional-disaster recovery.

**Latency vs. residency vs. resilience** is the trade triangle: closer regions cut latency; staying in one geography satisfies law; spreading across zones (and sometimes a second region) buys resilience. You rarely max all three — you choose.

## In practice

The location decision is made at the resource group and resource level:

```bash
# See which regions your subscription can use
az account list-locations --query "[].{name:name, display:displayName}" -o table

# Deploy near your users (latency) and in-country (residency)
az group create --name <rg-name> --location <region>   # e.g. westeurope

# For resilience, pick a zone-enabled region and spread instances
# across zones 1, 2, 3 (service-specific flag, illustrative):
az vm create --name <vm> --resource-group <rg-name> --zone 1 --image <image>
```

Rule of thumb: **region for latency + law, zones for resilience, a second region for disaster recovery.**

## Gotchas

- **A region is not automatically highly available.** Deploying to one region in one zone means one building's failure can take you down. Resilience is something you *opt into* by spreading across zones.
- **Not every region has zones, and not every service is zone-aware.** Check zone support for your region *and* your specific service before promising an SLA.
- **Don't assume your region has a pair.** The classic region-pair model is being supplemented by zone-first guidance and non-paired regions — verify in the docs rather than relying on old AZ-900 lore.
- **Geography is a legal boundary, not a performance one.** Picking a far-away region for compliance can cost you latency; that tension is the architecture, not a bug.
teachItBack: Azure's infrastructure nests geographies, regions, availability zones, and datacenters, and you choose a location to trade off three physical realities: latency (deploy near users), data residency (stay within a geography's legal boundary), and resilience (spread across availability zones). A region is just a metro group of datacenters and is not highly available on its own — zones are how you survive a building failure, and a second region is for regional disaster recovery. The old region-pair model still matters for some services, but 2025 guidance leans on availability zones first.

glossary.definition: A discrete market (often a country) that forms a data-residency and compliance boundary containing one or more regions.
A set of datacenters within a metro-area latency perimeter; the location you select when deploying a resource.
One or more datacenters within a region with independent power, cooling, and networking; zone-enabled regions have at least three.
Two regions in the same geography linked for staggered updates and geo-redundancy; increasingly supplemented by zone-first designs and non-paired regions.
